coredata.dc:title: Plasma-engineered graphene quantum dot-based nanocomposites as smart CO2-philic membranes with extremely high separation performance
coredata.dc:creator: Yeh, Yi-Jui
Lin, Wei
Chiang, Wei-Hung
Tung, Kuo-Lun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dioxide (CO2) capture, separation and utilization with membrane-based technologies are important for the development of carbon neutral society. However, conventional membranes are facing the difficulties of energy-consuming fabrication, expensive and toxic chemicals, and low efficient performance. Here, we report the CO2-Philic, optical-active nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)-embedded polyether-block-amide (Pebax)-based membranes for highly efficient CO2 separation and sensitive on-line CO2 detection. The NGQDs with abundant surface functionalities are synthesized from bioresource chitosan using a customized microplasma process. The plasma-engineered NGQDs with defined zero-dimensional (0D) geometry, surface functional groups and nitrogen dopants can enhance the CO2-philicity for CO2 capture and separation. Moreover, the NGQDs can not only generate the angstrom (Å)-level channels, but also tune the morphologies, pore structures, crystallinities of the nanophases, and functional groups of the Pebax-based membranes, leading to extremely high CO2 separation performance with a 415 Barrer CO2 permeability and a 125 CO2/N2 selectivity. Furthermore, the stable solid-state PL emission of the fabricated GQD@Pebax membranes enables the sensitive on-line monitoring of CO2 concentrations during the CO2 separation. Our work provides an understanding of the CO2 capture and transport with nanomaterials-based composites and a plasma engineering of advanced functional materials for clean energy, environmental and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plasma engineering
Graphene quantum dot
Nanocomposite
Carbon capture
Gas monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Gas separation study of GQD@Pebax membranes
Theoretical calculation study of GQD@Pebax nanocomposite membranes
In situ PL spectroscopy study of GQD@Pebax membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (NSTC Grant no. 111-2223-E-011-002-MY3 , 111-2628-E-011-002-MY2 , 109-2923-E-011-003-MY3 , 109-2221-E-002-102-MY3 , 111-2622-E-002-005 , 111-2634-F-002-016 , 112-2218-E-002-051 and 111-2221-E-002-015-MY3 ), Ministry of Education’s Higher Education Sprout Project under the Featured Area Research Center Program (112L9006), and Ministry of Education ("Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development Center" (SEED) Project). We also thank Ms. Chia-Ying Chien of MOST (National Taiwan University) for the assistance in FE-TEM experiment.

This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (NSTC Grant no. 111-2223-E-011-002-MY3, 111-2628-E-011-002-MY2, 109-2923-E-011-003-MY3, 109-2221-E-002-102-MY3, 111-2622-E-002-005, 111-2634-F-002-016, 112-2218-E-002-051 and 111-2221-E-002-015-MY3), Ministry of Education's Higher Education Sprout Project under the Featured Area Research Center Program (112L9006), and Ministry of Education (“Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development Center” (SEED) Project). We also thank Ms. Chia-Ying Chien of MOST (National Taiwan University) for the assistance in FE-TEM experiment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and architecture of Pebax and GQD@Pebax membranes. SEM (1) and AFM (2) images of (a) Pebax and (b) GQD0.08@Pebax membranes using the drop-casting process. (c) The thickness of GQDx@Pebax membranes with different NGQD loading. (d) The roughness value Ra and Rq of GQDx@Pebax membranes with different NGQD loading.

Crystalline structures and chemical bonds of the pristine Pebax and GQD@Pebax membranes. (a) DSC, (b) XRD, (c) FTIR, (d) wide scan XPS survey, and (e) C1s XPS spectra of pristine Pebax and GQD@Pebax membranes with different NGQD loading concentrations.

CO2 separation performance of GQD@Pebax membranes. (a) CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity of the as-fabricated MMM with various GQD loading weights (single gas, 1 bar, 25 °C). (b) Impact of input gas pressure on CO2 membrane permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity. (c) Comparative examination of the CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity of several Pebax-based membranes relative to the optimal filler content. (The upper limit is indicated by the pink line [59]). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Computational simulation study of GQD@Pebax composites membrane. (a) The illustrations of models for Pebax-1657 chain, NGQD, and GQD0.08@Pebax nanocomposite. (b) RDF of adhesion functional groups on the as-fabricated GQD0.08 MMM. (Find out the possible densities of the atoms of CO2 molecule at a predetermined radius.) (c) The calculated permeability and the selectivity of CO2/N2 of GQD@Pebax MMMs with different GQD mass loadings.

Monitoring CO2 study with GQD@Pebax MMMs via PL spectroscopy. (a) The illustration of on-line PL-based monitoring of CO2 concentrations during the CO2 capture and separation. (b) Steady-state PL spectra of GQD MMMs with different CO2 concentrations. (c) Stability test for GQD MMMs.

CO2-philic and optical-active plasma-engineered bioresource-derived GQD@Pebax mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) for extremely elevated CO2 separation and sensitive on-line CO2 detection. The plasma-engineered NGQDs with defined 0D geometry, surface functional groups and nitrogen dopants are designed and prepared to enhance the CO2-philicity by tuning the angstrom (Å)-level nanochannels, crystallinities of the nanophases, and functional groups of the Pebax-based membranes, leading to an extremely high CO2 separation efficiency. Moreover, the stable solid-state PL emission of the fabricated GQD@Pebax membranes enables the reproducible and sensitive on-line monitoring of CO2 concentrations during the CO2 separation, making them useful materials for industrial-oriented applications including carbon capture and separation, clean energy, environmental and biomedical applications.

head.ce:title: Plasma-engineered graphene quantum dot-based nanocomposites as smart CO2-philic membranes with extremely high separation performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10607, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607 Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607 Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Research Center for Green Materials Science and Technology, National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Research Center for Green Materials Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Research Center for Green Materials Science and Technology, National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Water Innovation, Low Carbon and Environmental Sustainability Research Center (WInnER Center), National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Low Carbon and Environmental Sustainability Research Center (WInnER Center)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Water Innovation, Low Carbon and Environmental Sustainability Research Center (WInnER Center), National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dioxide (CO2) capture, separation and utilization with membrane-based technologies are important for the development of carbon neutral society. However, conventional membranes are facing the difficulties of energy-consuming fabrication, expensive and toxic chemicals, and low efficient performance. Here, we report the CO2-Philic, optical-active nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)-embedded polyether-block-amide (Pebax)-based membranes for highly efficient CO2 separation and sensitive on-line CO2 detection. The NGQDs with abundant surface functionalities are synthesized from bioresource chitosan using a customized microplasma process. The plasma-engineered NGQDs with defined zero-dimensional (0D) geometry, surface functional groups and nitrogen dopants can enhance the CO2-philicity for CO2 capture and separation. Moreover, the NGQDs can not only generate the angstrom (Å)-level channels, but also tune the morphologies, pore structures, crystallinities of the nanophases, and functional groups of the Pebax-based membranes, leading to extremely high CO2 separation performance with a 415 Barrer CO2 permeability and a 125 CO2/N2 selectivity. Furthermore, the stable solid-state PL emission of the fabricated GQD@Pebax membranes enables the sensitive on-line monitoring of CO2 concentrations during the CO2 separation. Our work provides an understanding of the CO2 capture and transport with nanomaterials-based composites and a plasma engineering of advanced functional materials for clean energy, environmental and biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Global warming due to carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions is a critical environmental issue [1,2], and it is important to develop new technologies using advanced materials to separate, capture and reuse CO2 for the establishment of the carbon neutral society [3]. The membrane-enhanced methods for CO2 separation and capture have been shown to be effective to separate and purify CO2 in an industrial-scale manner [4,5]. Among the current developed membrane materials, the poly (ether-block-amide) based copolymers (commercially available as Pebax) composites show its high CO2 affinity and stable mechanical features, it represents a suitable substrate for the development of high-performance membranes for CO2 separation and capture. However, the conventional Pebax-based membranes for CO2 separation have been hampered by the difficulties of complex fabrication, expensive chemicals, uncontrollable nanostructures and low-efficient performance [6,7]. Moreover, it is challenging to monitor the CO2 separation efficiency with the optical methods during the separation because the current Pebax membrane materials are lacking optical properties for on-line monitoring. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop advanced functional materials with exceptional and controlled properties for the low-cost, efficient, and detectable membrane-based CO2 capture and separation [8–10].

To accomplish the primarily to facilitate, we develop photoluminescent (PL)-active nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)-enhanced Pebax-based mixed matrix membranes (denoted GQD@Pebax MMMs) for effective CO2 separation and sensitive on-line monitoring. Nanomaterials such as one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes (CNT) [11], nanowires [12], and two-dimensional (2D) graphene [13] and Mxene nanosheets [14] have been used as nanofillers of membranes to improve the performances. The GQDs with zero-dimensional (0D) nanostructures recently attract significant attention because of their exceptional properties including stable and controllable PL emissions, tunable surface functionalities, large surface areas, and biocompatibility, making them promising candidates for catalysis [15,16], clean energy [17], and biomedical applications [18]. Moreover, GQDs can be applied as the nanocrosslinkers and fluorescent fillers in polymeric composites, providing advanced properties to nanocomposites for separation [19,20] and on-line detection applications [21]. By heteroatom doping such as nitrogen (N) atoms, the GQDs can exhibit enhanced PL properties, controllable surface interaction with polar molecules, and chemical reactivity with polymers, further improving the applications performances [22–25].

Current methods to produce NGQDs include the top-down and bottom-up approaches [15,26,27]. The top-down methods usually require the dissociation of expensive N-doped carbon materials including graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon fibers by oxidation, electrochemical, or mechanical exfoliation in harsh conditions. On the other hand, the bottom-up methods typically use carbon and N-containing compounds to synthesize NGQDs through precise and complicated carbonization processes, pyrolysis, or sequential chemical pathways. In fact, both procedures require toxic reagents and chemicals, lengthy and arduous approaches, costly equipment, and extreme heat. [22,23]. Nonthermal and low-temperature plasma-based technologies especially microplasma engineering is an efficient and green method to produce NGQDs without the need of toxic chemicals, high temperature and sophisticated vacuum system [28,29].

Here, we report the plasma-engineered NGQD-enhanced Pebax-based nanocomposites as the smart CO2-philic membranes with extremely high separation performance of CO2 and stable solid-state PL properties for sensitive on-line CO2 detection. The NGQDs with monodispersed nanometer size and controlled surface functionalities were synthesized with earth-abundant bioresource chitosan using customized microplasma process (Scheme 1 ). The unique plasma-engineered 0D geometry, surface functional groups and nitrogen dopants of NGQDs can improve the CO2-philicity to improve the CO2 adsorption. Importantly, the solubility and diffusivity of CO2 can be highly enhanced by the molecular hybridization of NGQDs with Pebax polymer chains in the membranes based on the theoretical calculation. Moreover, the NGQDs can not only assist to generate the angstrom (Å)-level channels, but also modify the morphologies, pore structures, crystallinities of the nanophases, and functional groups of the Pebax membranes, leading to exceptional CO2 separation efficiency with a 415 Barrer CO2 permeability and a 125 CO2/N2 selectivity. Furthermore, the stable solid-state PL emission of the fabricated GQD@Pebax membranes enables the sensitive on-line monitoring of CO2 concentrations during the CO2 separation. Our work provides a fundamental understating of the CO2 capture and transport using nanomaterials-based composites and a plasma manufacturing of advanced functional materials for clean energy, environmental and biomedical applications.

The fabrication of GQD@Pebax MMMs utilizing solution casting procedures in several steps. The GQDs were added to a solvent composed of 60 % ethanol and 40 % DI water, sonicated for 40 min, and then mixed for 12 hours at room temperature using a super mixer. After completely mixing the solution, add 1 wt% of Pebax-1657 polymer (3 g Pebax dissolved in 30 g solvent). To distribute the GQDs evenly throughout the Pebax polymer, the solution was heated to 75 degrees Celsius and agitated for an additional 180 min. After dissolving Pebax-based nanocomposite, the solution with 2 mL was drop-cast into a PVDF-supported membrane and ambient-dried for 12 hours at room temperature. Keep the as-fabricated membrane in a vacuum oven for a further 12 hours to evaporate any remnants of solvent.

Through the use of an interior metal penetration device with an active area of 3.5 cm2, gas permeation tests were conducted. CO2 and N2 pass through via membranes and porous metal discs. with varying transmembrane pressures (1–6 bar) at room temperature. Determine the mean of the data collected from the produced samples. To measure the velocity of permeate gas, soap film flow meters are used. The mixed matrix membrane (MMM) was carefully placed within a permeation cell, after which the system underwent a 24-hour evacuation to remove any residual gases before initiating the separation procedure [30–32]. The equations were used to compute the gas permeable (P) and appropriate selectivity (S).
P =
Qt
pA
. Q is the gas flow rate (cm3/s) and A is the film thickness. P is the steady membrane pressure, measured in cmHg and ranging from 1 to 6 bar. This research defines ideal selectivity as the ratio of gas “a” to gas “b”; in this instance, “a” is CO2 and “b” is N2. Consequently, the optimal selectivity may be computed using the formula.
α =
Pa
Pb

The PL solid measurement exhibits exceptional sensitivity and precision in quantifying CO2 levels via quantum dots. In the conducted experiments, CO2 was injected into the designated CO2 chamber, Gas samples are then extracted and gas data are analyzed using gas chromatography. The light induced by the quantum material was also analyzed using PL solid measurement to emission spectroscopy. This process was performed continuously over a duration of 60 min to accurately assess CO2 concentrations.

Utilizing molecular modeling, the influence of GQDs on Pebax membranes was analyzed. A comparison was made between the GQD@pebax-1657 MMM model and the original Pebax-1657 membrane model. The chemical composition of the membrane, its affinity for carbon dioxide, and the passage of gas molecules have all been meticulously studied. All simulations and calculations were performed using Materials Studio 2017R2 (BIOVIA, San Diego). The self-diffusivity of each gas molecule was determined using the mean-squared displacement. 100 CO2/N2 molecules were introduced to the membrane for a 3000 ps MD simulation. Einstein's connection [38] may be applied to the MSD plot's linear relationship with time.

The NGQDs were initially synthesized using a customized microplasma method. The plasma-synthesized NGQDs exhibit stable PL emission and dispersion in aqueous solution (Fig 0.1(a)). The Supplemental Materials provide comprehensive descriptions of NGQD manufacturing and analysis. (Fig. S1–5 and Section Fig. S2–4). The GQDx@Pebax membranes with varying nominal of NGQD mass loading × (0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08 and 0.10 wt%) were then fabricated using a modified casting process (details in the Experimental Section). The surface morphologies of the GQD@Pebax membranes were first analyzed by the atomic force microscopy (AFM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 1 and Fig. S6). AFM images show that the roughness of the GQD@Pebax membranes are increased with the increased NGQD concentrations (Fig. 1(c-2) and Fig. S6) On the other hand, the pristine Pebax membrane exhibits a thin membrane (thickness ∼ 3 μm) with smooth morphology (Fig. 1 (b-1)). After adding NGQDs into the Pebax membranes, more surface roughness is observed, and GQD agglomerate appears on the membrane surfaces (Fig. 1 (c-1), Fig. S6). Cross-sectional SEM images indicate that the thicknesses of GQD@Pebax membranes are in the range of 3 to 9 μm, and the thicknesses of the membranes are increased with the increased NGQD concentrations. (Fig. 1(c) and Fig. S7) The expansion of the membrane thickness can be attributed to steric hindrance and functional group interactions between the incorporated nanofillers and the Pebax matrix. Notably, the presence of hydrophilic GQD nanofillers induces the formation of voids within the pebax-based film, resulting in a looser film structure and an increase in selective membrane thickness [20,33–35]. Both the SEM and AFM observations indicate that the NGQDs can change the microstructures and surface morphologies of the prepared GQD@Pebax membranes, which should affect the CO2 transport in the membranes.

The crystallinity of the polymer-based membranes is an important parameter to affect the CO2 transport in the membranes [36–38]. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was subsequently employed to investigate the crystalline phases of the GQD@Pebax membranes. As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and Fig. S8, the pristine Pebax membrane possesses 13.01 % and 16.22 % crystallinities for the PA6 and PEO nanophases, respectively. It is also noted that several non-crystallized segments were existed within the Pebax membranes based on the DSC result. Interestingly, the crystallinities of PA6 polyamide and PEO nanophases of Pebax matrix were found to be increased and decreased, respectively with increased concentrations of NGQDs in the GQD@Pebax membranes. The result suggests that the NGQDs can tune the crystallinities of PA6 polyamide and PEO nanophases of Pebax-based MMM and then controlling the CO2 solubility and transport in the Pebax matrix. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was further performed to confirm the crystallization and nanostructures of the GQD@Pebax membranes. Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD patterns of pristine Pebax and prepared GQD@Pebax membranes with different NGQD concentrations. The (002) diffraction peak of Pebax matrix located at 20.5° was red-shifted with the addition of NGQDs, suggesting that the NGQDs can reduce the interlayer spacing of the Pebax polymer matrix. The above DSC and XRD results indicate that the crystalline structures of the Pebax matrix can be modified by the NGQDs, which is in agreement with the SEM observation.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were additionally used to analyze the chemical environments of the GQD@Pebax membranes. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the Pebax and GQD@Pebax membranes all exhibit chemical bands located at 1040, 1540, 1635, and 3270 cm−1, which are ascribed to C-N, N-H, C = O, and OH functional groups, respectively [39,40]. It is found that the peak intensities of C = O, C-N, and OH were increased with increased NGQD concentrations, suggesting that the functional groups of the Pebax matrix can be modified with the NGQDs. The XPS spectra further suggest that the N and O contents in the GQD@Pebax membranes were increased with the increased NGQD concentrations (Fig. 2(d) and Table S2). Moreover, the XPS C1s spectrum of GQD@Pebax membranes reveals the presence of the C = O (286.7 eV), C-N (284.6 eV), and C = C groups (283.2 eV) (Fig. 2(e) and Table S3) [41,42]. The bonds in the C1s XPS spectrum show the shifting to higher binding energy, suggesting the crosslinking of NGQDs with the Pebax matrix Overall, the FTIR and XPS data indicate that the concentrations of C = O and C-N bonds were enhanced with increased NGQD concentrations, suggesting that the chemical functionalities of the Pebax membranes can be modified by the NGQDs. It is explained that the rich surface functional groups and N dopants of NGQDs can interact with the functional groups of Pebax polymer chains and create new chemical environments with different CO2 affinities. Moreover, the CO2 affinities of Pebax matrix can be tuned by adjusting the NGQD loading concentrations. Based on the above characterization including SEM, AFM, DSC, XPS, FTIR and XPS, it is suggested that the surface morphologies, crystalline structures, and chemical functionalities of the composite membranes can be tuned by adjusting the concentrations of the NGQDs in the Pebax matrix. The fabricated low-cost (Section S5.) GQD@Pebax membranes with different NGQD concentrations can be used to study the CO2 affinity and transport in the Pebax membranes for understanding the separation mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas separation study of GQD@Pebax membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A detailed single-gas measurement was performed to evaluate the gas separation performance of the as-fabricated matrix membranes. Details of the measurements are provided in the Experimental section and Supporting Information. Fig. 3 (a) shows the testing result of the pristine Pebax membranes and fabricated GQD@Pebax membranes with different NGQD loading concentrations. Firstly, the CO2 permeability of pure Pebax membranes was 93 Barrer and the CO2/N2 selectivity was 44. Comparatively, all GQD@Pebax membranes exhibited superior CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity, demonstrating that NGQD may increase the CO2 collection and separation efficiency of Pebax membranes. Particularly, the GQD0.08@Pebax membrane had an outstanding outcome, with a CO2 permeability of 415 Barrer and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 125. This results might be considered to be the consequence of improved nanophase separation, which permits a more flexible bulk PEO chain, hence increasing the availability of CO2 affinity sites and decreasing transport barrier. Nevertheless, when the quantity of NGQD was raised to 0.10 wt%, the permeability of CO2 decreased, most likely as a result of NGQD aggregation in the Pebax matrix. The influence of working pressure on gas separation performance was studied in order to comprehend gas movement during swelling. As the GQD0.08@Pebax membrane had the best CO2 separation performance, it was used as the control was the Pebax membrane. As demonstrated in Fig. 3(b), when the pressure was raised from 1 to 6 bar, the manufactured GQD0.08@Pebax membrane displayed a reduction in CO2 permeability and an increase in CO2/N2 selectivity. With increased input gas pressure, the known “Carrier Saturation Effect” reduces the gas permeance and enhances the selectivity of the membranes [43]. In addition, the selectivity of reflectance current gas pressure may be related to the compressibility of the membrane.

In comparing the above CO2 separation performance of GQD@Pebax membranes with previous work, there is a broad variety of optimal concentrations of the nanofillers in the Pebax-based membranes and most nanofillers show optimal concentrations below 2 % (Fig. 3(c) and Table S4) [13,44–65]. Fig. 3(c) demonstrates a trade-off diagram with selectivity and permeance with Robeson's upper limitations [66,67]. In our work, the CO2 separation performance of the fabricated GQD@Pebax membrane exceeds the upper limits by adding 0.08 wt% of NGQDs in the Pebax membranes. The exceptional performance can be linked to the NGQD-induced pore structure and enhanced CO2 affinities by the NGQD surface functional groups and N dopants. Overall, the findings suggest that NGQDs with plasma-engineered size and surface functionalities can regulate the nanophases and chemical configurations of Pebax membranes with more precision than other nanofillers by the GQD-induced molecular-level hybridization. In addition to adjusting the nanocrystalline structure of the matrix polymer, the incorporation of GQD nanofillers efficiently modifies the hydrophilicity of the entire matrix membrane and generates sub-angstrom pore channels. The involvement of sub-3 nm of nanofiller size results in extremely high levels of CO2 capture and separation performance.

Additional studies were carried out to determine the stability of the permeation performance of the GQD0.08@Pebax membrane at different feed gas pressures (Fig.S9). The CO2 permeability and selectivity demonstrated minor variations at different feed gas pressures, but remained continuously stable over the whole 120-hour testing period. This encompassed exposure to feed gas pressures at 1 bar, 2 bar, 4 bar, and 6 bar. The slight observed variations do not undermine the overall stability of the CO2 permeability and selectivity in the composite membranes. The aforementioned result emphasizes the notable durability of the composite membrane when subjected to various operational pressures, hence demonstrating its dependability in real-world scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation study of GQD@Pebax nanocomposite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of NGQDs in the GQD@Pebax membranes on the CO2 separation performance was systematically studied by theoretical calculation. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), each polymer chain consists of twenty superunits. The radial distribution functions (RDFs) of different NGQD functional groups were analyzed to study the interaction of CO2 molecules with NGQD functional groups in details. Fig. 4(b) displays the RDFs of four functional groups on NGQDs and Pebax chains for CO2 molecule attraction. All of the individual peaks concentrate at a distance of 3 or 4 nm between CO2 molecules and functional groups, suggesting that physical adsorption is existed. The C = O functional group of NGQDs shows the greatest intensity which is about three times more appealing for CO2 than the Pebax C-O-C functional group, suggesting that the NGQDs exhibit significant affinity for CO2. Fig. 4(c) shows the simulated CO2 separation efficiency of the as-fabricated MMM membranes with different NGQD loading concentrations. It has been shown that CO2 permeability and selectivity increase by NGQD load upto to 0.08 wt%, but reduced significantly with NGQD loading of 0.10 wt%. It is noticed that the CO2 permeability rose a bit but the N2 permeability increased greatly with 0.10 wt% NGQD loading, and therefore there is a decrease in selectivity. Simulations demonstrate that high NGQD concentrations can boost N2 transport while saturation of CO2 absorption and a strong affinity for CO2 constrain CO2 diffusion happen. As NGQD mass loading is increased, the CO2 diffusivity is more critical than the CO2 permeability. Overall, the simulated permeability in Fig. 4(c) matches experimental data in Fig. 3(a) and Fig S10.

The solubility of CO2 is affected by the relative strength of the affinities of NGQDs and Pebax functional groups based on the result. The RDF analysis suggests that adding NGQDs into the Pebax matrix can enhance membrane affinity for CO2 [68]. It properly describes sorption loading and behavior (Detailed information in S6). Fig. S11 exhibits membrane solubility and CO2/N2 sorption selectivity. NGQD greatly enhances CO2 solubility with practically unchanged N2. GQD@Pebax is a solubility-dominated membrane because CO2 is more soluble than N2, with a selectivity of 110.5. However, high NGQD concentrations diminish selectivity and eliminate CO2 solubility. The N2 diffusivity is somewhat greater than CO2 because of its smaller size (Fig S12). The ratio of diffusion selectivity of pure Pebax is close to 1. With NGQDs, N2 diffusivity increases thrice whereas CO2 diffusivity decreases. The NGQD constructs an experimental N2 transport tunnel. With large NGQD loading, diffusivity selectivity reduces greatly. Nanotunnels boost N2 transport, but too much NGQD inhibits CO2 diffusivity despite the membrane increases affinity for CO2. A saturation effect on CO2 affinity can happen with high NGQD loading in the Pebax matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ PL spectroscopy study of GQD@Pebax membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PL spectroscopy was carried out to study the PL-based detection of CO2 with different concentrations using the fabricated GQD@Pebax MMMs [69], as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a). Fig. 5(b) shows the steady-state PL spectra of GQD MMMs with different CO2 concentrations. The PL intensities of the GQD membranes are gradually decreased with increasing CO2 concentrations, suggesting that the PL emissions of NGQDs are quenched by the absorbed CO2. Importantly, a linear relationship between the PL intensity quench ratio, I/I0, and CO2 concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 ppm is established with the fabricated GQD0.08@Pebax MMMs, where I and I0 denote the PL intensities of GQD0.08@Pebax MMMs with and without CO2 adsorption, respectively. The linearity can be expressed as y = -0.9994x + 0.9984 with a 0.99561 R2 value, where y and x represent the I/I0, and CO2 concentrations, respectively. Moreover, the limit of detection (LoD) of 2.2 ppm CO2 concentration can be determined using the triple standard deviation (3σ) method, the PL intensity ratio can be sensitive to variations in CO2 concentration (Fig. S13). We further continuously tested the on-line CO2 detection with CO2 concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 ppm for 5 runs. Between the two cycles, the membrane was reused without any treatments. It is found that the on-line CO2 detection performances were maintained without apparent change, indicating that the fabricated GQD@Pebax MMMs possess stable recyclability and CO2 capture ability. Overall, the results show that the fabricated GQD@Pebax MMMs can be applied to on-line monitoring of the CO2 concentration in a rapid and sensitive manner using PL measurement.

CO2 capture, separation and utilization are not only important for industrial applications and fundamental study but also critical for the establishment of environmentally safe and sustainable society. However, current membrane-based technologies for CO2 capture and separation are hampered by the problems of low time- and energy-efficient performances, toxic chemicals and costly fabrication. In this work, we have developed a simple, facile and scalable plasma engineering to fabricate optical-active NGQD-enabled Pebax-based membranes with extremely high CO2-philicity and stable solid-state PL emissions for efficient CO2 separation and sensitive on-line CO2 monitoring. Detailed experimental and calculation studies reveal that the plasma-engineered NGQDs with 0D nanostructures, rich surface functional groups and N dopants can enhance the CO2-philicity by optimizing the morphologies, pore structures, crystallinities of the nanophases, and functional groups of the Pebax membrane matrix, owning a superior CO2 permeability of 415 Barrer and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 125. The stable solid-state PL emission of the fabricated GQD@Pebax membranes enables the sensitive on-line monitoring of CO2 concentrations with the concentration from 0 ppm to 450 ppm during the CO2 separation. Our work opens a new avenue for advanced functional materials fabrication for gas capture and separation, catalysis, clean environmental and biomedicine applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (NSTC Grant no. 111-2223-E-011-002-MY3, 111-2628-E-011-002-MY2, 109-2923-E-011-003-MY3, 109-2221-E-002-102-MY3, 111-2622-E-002-005, 111-2634-F-002-016, 112-2218-E-002-051 and 111-2221-E-002-015-MY3), Ministry of Education’s Higher Education Sprout Project under the Featured Area Research Center Program (112L9006), and Ministry of Education ("Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development Center" (SEED) Project). We also thank Ms. Chia-Ying Chien of MOST (National Taiwan University) for the assistance in FE-TEM experiment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.146547.

ce:section.ce:para:
